profile.summary: A Senior Engineer owns delivery of complex technical work, shapes implementation choices, and raises code quality across a team. They usually partner with product, design, and other engineers, while mentoring less experienced teammates and handling ambiguous problems. Scope is often a service, system area, or major feature rather than whole-org technical direction.

known_challenges.text: Balancing hands-on delivery with mentoring, reviews, and unblocking others.
Turning vague product goals into workable technical plans without overengineering.
Carrying informal leadership without always having clear authority or decision rights.
Context switching across coding, incidents, meetings, and support work drains focus.
Being relied on for hard problems can create constant interruption and pressure.

recommended_question_themes.why: Senior engineers are expected to make sound tradeoffs and need clarity on what they truly own.
Their impact often depends on how well they help others grow and move faster.
They are often the default escalation point, which can quietly erode focus and energy.
This level regularly has to balance speed, quality, maintainability, and risk.
Much of the friction at this level comes from translating between technical constraints and product needs.

terminology.meaning: Peer feedback on code before merging.
Improve code structure without changing behavior.
Past shortcuts that slow future change.
Automated build, test, and deployment flow.
Logs, metrics, and traces to understand systems.
Planning components, data flow, and tradeoffs.
Choice that improves one thing at a cost.
Being accountable for outcomes in an area.
Removing obstacles so others can progress.
Written plan for a technical approach.
Short delivery cycle for planned work.
Ordered list of work to do.
Work expands beyond the original plan.
Other work or teams needed to proceed.

listen_for: Whether they talk about outcomes and tradeoffs, not just tasks completed.
How often they mention being interrupted, pulled into fixes, or acting as escalation.
Whether they describe mentoring as rewarding, invisible, or draining.
Shifts from confident technical language to vagueness around priorities or ownership.
Whether they frame problems at code level only or connect them to product impact.
avoid: Assuming senior means they want to become a people manager soon.
Treating them as a ticket closer instead of someone shaping technical direction.
Piling on mentoring and support work without adjusting delivery expectations.
Mistaking calm competence for low ambition or low need for support.
